content.full_text:
JUN 12 '90 15:15 LCC-515/444-5570/1                                P.2/8


                        League of California Cities
                                                    Sacramento, California
                                                    June 12, 1990

VIA TELECOPY

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     Pursuant to section 18329 of title 2 of the California Code of Regulations, the
League of California Cities is writing to request the advice of the Fair Political Practices
Commission on whether local elected officials may use their campaign funds to join,
along with their spouses, the League's official study mission to Eastern Europe.

                        About the League of California Cities

     The League is writing as a representative of California cities and their elected
officials, including its President, Iola Williams, who also serves as a city council member
in San Jose.  See 2 Cal. Code of Reg. § 18329(b)(1). The League is a statewide
association of California cities dedicated to promoting the interests of California cities.

     As part of this effort, the League sponsors numerous conferences each year on
issues facing cities and their officials. Of course, many of these issues transcend state
and even national boundaries. Recognizing this, the California League and several of its
members are active in the National League of Cities. In addition, the League has
sponsored these international study missions, when the need has arisen, over the past
twenty or so years.

                            About the Study Mission

     The purpose of the mission is for California city officials to meet with Eastern
European city officials in Warsaw, Prague and East Berlin. The California city officials
hope to share information and provide support to their Eastern European counterparts,
as those officials attempt to cope with the extraordinary political and economic
transitions their countries are undergoing.

     To familiarize California city officials with the circumstances in Eastern European
cities, there will be some touring in addition to the traveling to and from meeting
destinations. A copy of the preliminary itinerary is attached. Some city officials may
have scheduled other, individual meetings with government and diplomatic officials
which are not reflected on the preliminary itinerary.


CONFERENCE REGISTRATION OFFICE                    HEADQUARTERS                          SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA OFFICE
BOX 7006, LAFAYETTE, CA 94549              1400 K STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814          404 HILTON CENTER OFFICE BLDG.
(415) 283-7135                                (916) 444-5790                            900 WILSHIRE BOULEVARD
                                                                                          LOS ANGELES, CA 90017

[illegible]

[illegible]

[illegible]

[illegible]

JUN 12 '90 15:16 LCC-316/444-9671                                P.3/8


          As with any League-sponsored event, the gathering of California city officials also
enables California city officials to share information informally among themselves about
the challenges facing them back home.

                                          Analysis

          According to the League's research, California Government Code section 85801
allows elected officials to use their campaign funds for expenditures associated with
holding office, as long as the expenditures are reasonably related to a legislative or
governmental purpose. See Cal. Gov't Code § 85801. Campaign funds may be used to
pay for travel expenses and necessary accommodations as long as these expenditures are
"directly related" to a governmental function. See Cal. Gov't Code § 85802(a).

          Under the Government Code, members of the elected officials' households may
accompany officials to the same destination using campaign funds, if the elected officials
may do so. See Cal. Gov't Code § 85802(a)(2)(household members' travel "shall be
considered for the same purpose as the . . . elected officer's travel"). The League notes
that spouses are always encouraged to accompany local officials to League events, out of
a recognition that spouses frequently play an important role in the local officials'
discharge of their public and League responsibilities. Moreover, the League's Board of
Directors' Manual specifically provides an allowance for travel expenses incurred when
the Board President's spouse travels with the President on League business inside or
outside of California.

          Payments for travel and necessary accommodations are considered "directly
related" to a governmental purpose if the payments would meet standards "similar to"
the standards in Sections 162 and 274 of the Internal Revenue Code for deductions of
travel expenses. See Cal. Gov't Code § 85802(a)(1).

          Section 162(a)(2) of the Internal Revenue Code allows taxpayers to deduct
traveling expenses, including amounts expended on meals and lodging, while away from
home in the pursuit of a trade or business. 26 U.S.C § 162(a)(2). Those traveling
expenses which are reasonable and necessary in the conduct of the taxpayer's business
and directly attributable to it may be deducted. See 26 C.F.R. § 1.162-2.

          The term "trade or business" in the Internal Revenue Code includes the
performance of the functions of a public official. 26 U.S.C. § 7701(a)(26). Thus, the
Internal Revenue Code allows elected officials to deduct traveling expenses which are
reasonable and necessary to their functions as a public official.

          Section 274 of the Internal Revenue Code allows deductions for foreign travel for
that portion of expenses which is allocable to such trade or business functions of a
public official. See 26 U.S.C. § 274(c)(1) (". . . no deduction shall be allowed under
section 162 . . . for that portion of the expenses of such travel otherwise allowable under
such section which, under regulations prescribed by the Secretary, is not allocable to
such trade or business for functions of a public official . . ."). Thus, public officials'
travel expenses are deductible under the Internal Revenue Code, as long as the officials
follow the allocation rules set forth in section 1.274-4 of title 26 of the Code of Federal
Regulations (relating to disallowance of certain kinds of foreign travel expenses).


                                            2


JUN 12 '90 15:17 LCC-210/444-2671                                P.4/5


        The Fair Political Practices Commission has recognized in the past that
attendance at conferences and educational programs directly relate to the performance
of local officials' governmental duties. See Request for Advice No. A-90-210 (March 27,
1990) (Board of Education member may use campaign funds to pay travel and tuition
expenses for Harvard's "Program for Senior Executives in State and Local
Government"); Request for Advice No. A-90-308 (May 30, 1990) (Board of Education
member may use campaign funds for travel, accommodations and conference fees for
conference sponsored by both the California and Hawaii middle schools associations on
middle schools).

        As in those two cases, the League's official study mission provides local officials
with an opportunity to expand their horizons on local government issues. In addition,
the trip has the philanthropic purpose of enabling California's local officials to share
their experiences and insights on local government issues with officials overseas, many of
whom are grappling with these issues for the first time. The mission is therefore a
natural extension of the League's motto that "California Cities Work Together."

                                        Conclusion

        In summary, the League would greatly appreciate the Fair Political Practices
Commission's opinion on this issue. The League would also appreciate the
Commission's views on whether it would strengthen a local official's position for his or
her city council to pass a resolution affirming the official nature of the trip and its
benefits to the cities who send officials and those which host them.

        Unfortunately, the registration deadline for the study mission is June 30, 1990.
Accordingly, it would be extremely helpful if the Commission could provide an initial,
oral opinion (which the League realizes is non-binding on the Commission) and then
follow up such an opinion with a written opinion.

        Thank you for the Commission's prompt attention to this request. The League's
General Counsel, JoAnne Speers, will contact the Commission on Friday, June 15, 1990
regarding the Commission's initial views on this issue. Please do not hesitate to call her
if you have any questions regarding this request.


                                                Very truly yours,

                                                [signature]
                                                Don Benninghoven
                                                Executive Director

I can see this document has yellow highlighted text, but the text itself is not legible enough for me to transcribe accurately. The image shows several lines of yellow highlighting on a white background, but the underlying text is too unclear or faded for me to read the specific words with confidence.

To provide an accurate transcription following your rules, I would need a clearer image where the text is readable.

THE LEAGUE OF
CALIFORNIA CITIES

Official Study Mission to

EASTERN EUROPE

September 30 - October 13, 1990

WARSAW • BUDAPEST • PRAGUE • EAST & WEST BERLIN

Seldom has there been a time so full of hope
as we are now witnessing as nation after
nation in Eastern Europe is shaking off the
shackles of Stalinism. Borders are thrown open
from East Berlin to Prague for the first time
in four decades and all barriers are
coming down.

These are extraordinary times of political
and economical transition. As the cheering
stops, the countries of Eastern Europe will be
facing the grim realities of economic survival
in a competitive world. This is a time when
they will need the support of the West to help
them steer a course in their unchartered
economic waters and gradually catch up
with the world around them.

In order to contribute in some way, the
League of California Cities is organizing a
special study mission to visit Poland, Hungary,
Czechoslovakia and Berlin in October. There
will be meetings with city officials in Warsaw,
Prague and East Berlin as per itinerary enclosed.
We hope that many of our members will take
advantage of this opportunity of exchanges
with these beautiful and historic countries.

League Presidents                Executive Director

ITINERARY

30 September, Sunday — Depart from LOS ANGELES
Afternoon departure from Los Angeles International Airport on board Lufthansa Airlines for a non-stop, overnight flight to Frankfurt, West Germany.
(Ample meal service on board)

1 October, Monday — WARSAW, POLAND
Morning arrival in Frankfurt where we connect with a nonstop flight to Warsaw. Arriving in the mid-afternoon. Following immigration and customs formalities, transfer directly to our hotel.

2 October, Tuesday — IN WARSAW
Of all the countries in Eastern Europe, Poland is the largest, the poorest, the finest, and the most vibrant; a land of rebellious, indomitable, determined people. Occupied many times within the thousand years of its history, but never conquered or subjugated. This morning sightseeing tour of Warsaw. Poland's capital, largely rebuilt after World War II. This afternoon, MEETING WITH CITY OFFICIALS. (B)

3 October, Wednesday — IN WARSAW
This morning we leave for an excursion to ZELAZOWA WOLA, the birthplace of Frederic Chopin. We will enjoy lunch at a typical Polish restaurant before returning to Warsaw. (B)

4 October, Thursday — WARSAW to BUDAPEST, HUNGARY
Morning at leisure. Afternoon transfer to the airport for a short flight to Budapest. We arrive in the late afternoon and following immigration and customs formalities, we transfer directly to our hotel.

Budapest, capital of Hungary and known as the Little Paris on the Danube, is really two cities divided by the Danube River; on the right is the ancient, Hilly Buda, a charming area of medieval cobblestones streets and gothic arches, watchtowers and wine cellars; to the left, is the newer, livelier and bustling Pest, home to numerous smart shops, restaurants and cafes. Budapest is rich with cultural life with dozens of theatres, and concert halls, scores of romantic gardens, restaurants and nightclubs cabarets; a place of brilliantly exuberant "czardas" dancers and famous gypsy violinists so proficient at their soulful art that it is said you never have to tip them unless they make you cry. (B)

5 October, Friday — IN BUDAPEST
We begin our day with a Saturday sightseeing tour of this historical city over 2000 years old. Our tour will encompass both the modern life and the old sections of the city, Buda and Pest; St. Stephen's Cathedral; the Parliament Building; the Millennium Memorial with its breathtaking view of the Danube, the Castle District, the Fisherman's Bastion, Gellert Hill and the Citadel. This afternoon, MEETING WITH CITY OFFICIALS. (B)

6 October, Saturday — IN BUDAPEST
Today is all yours to enjoy this exciting city. Optional excursions will be offered. (B)

7 October, Sunday — BUDAPEST to PRAGUE, CZECHOSLOVAKIA
Very early morning transfer to the airport for the short flight to Prague. On arrival, immigration and custom for-

malities followed by a tour of the city on the way to our hotel. We will arrive in time for a late lunch.

Prague, the Capital of Czechoslovakia, stately "City of a Hundred Spires," Prague (or "Praha" as the Czecks call it) is the center of Czech history, once capital of Old Bohemia, presently seat of the President.

This beautiful city, set on five hills which stretch over the banks of the Vistula with its sumptuous ancient and modern bridges, is inhabited by more than one million people. Prague reflects the march of modernization; efficient transportation, magnificent shops with the best in Czech goods, craftsmanship, and lively evening entertainment. However, this modernization has been perfectly balanced by painstaking efforts to restore their historic buildings and monuments.

Yes, whether you come to do business in Prague, to study it as an educational center, or simply to explore its cultural heritage, you will find in this city a charming storybook charm that makes the greatest impact; For this is the city that portended Goethe in describing it as "the most beautiful jewel in the stone crown of the world". (B)

8 October, Monday — IN PRAGUE
This morning is at leisure.
In the afternoon, MEETING WITH CITY OFFICIALS. (B)

9 October, Tuesday — PRAGUE to EAST BERLIN, GERMAN DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC
Mid-day transfer to the airport for the flight to East Berlin, followed by immigration and customs formalities and transfer to our hotel.

There is nothing in Berlin's past that is as exciting as its present; no city of it seven hundred years of history is poignantly dramatic as its "wall" which has itself become suddenly an historical remnant of the Stalinist regime. (B)

10 October, Wednesday — IN EAST BERLIN
Our morning tour of East Berlin will take us to the reconstructed Old Palace, used today by Humboldt University as a teachers college; the State Opera House; the state library, the Opera Cafe, St. Hedwigs Cathedral; visit the reconstructed Alexander Platz, the TV Tower and Marienkirche. MEETING WITH CITY OFFICIALS. (B)

11 October, Thursday — EAST to WEST BERLIN Federal Republic of Germany
After breakfast, we'll experience the excitement of crossing from East to West Berlin. Here you will be awed by the contrasts between these two cities. Now you enter West Berlin, a peninsula, where the conventional exists side-by-side with the extraordinary. It is metropolis and Bohemia; small-town and old quarters; urbanity and rural tranquility—the perfect ending of your grand tour.

After a comprehensive tour of the city, we check into our hotel. (B)

12 October, Friday — IN WEST BERLIN
Day at leisure — do that last minute shopping while you further explore this exciting city. (B)

13 October, Sat. — WEST BERLIN to LOS ANGELES
Early morning transfer to the airport for a short flight to Frankfurt, where we will connect with Lufthansa non-stop flight to Los Angeles.
Arrival in Los Angeles is this same day at 2:15 P.M.


To provide an accurate transcription following your rules, I would need a clearer, higher resolution image where the text is readable.

TOUR PRICE PER PERSON: $3,875.00 (Based on double occupancy)

The Above Tour Price Includes:
• Air transportation via economy class from Los Angeles and all land
arrangements as per itinerary.
• $100,000 automatic flight insurance during all flight segments for which
the ticket was issued by DW Travel.
• First class hotels as specified or similar, based on double occupancy
with private bath. The supplement cost for a single room is $600.00.
• Buffet breakfast daily. Lunch in Zalizowa Wola.
• Professional meetings as per itinerary.
• Transfers between hotels and airports including baggage handling,
as per itinerary.
• All transportation during sightseeing tours and entertainment as men-
tioned in the itinerary.
• Gratuities to hotel staff and local government taxes on hotel bills.
• Assistance of a DW Travel Tour Director who will supervise the smooth
operation of the arrangements for the safety and comfort of our pas-
sengers.
• Custom name badge, baggage tags and tour document holder.
• All expenses in connection with the administration and operation of
the tour.

Not Included: Cost of passport and visas, airport departure taxes, excess
luggage charges, meals not specified in the inclusives, beverages, tele-
phone calls, tips to guides and drivers, and items of a personal nature
such as laundry, personal services and any other such items not
specified above.

— SEE REVERSE FOR RESERVATION FORM —

GENERAL INFORMATION

Important Note:
This tour and all its inclusions are based on a minimum
of 25 passengers travelling together throughout. Prices
are effective as of November 1, 1989 and are subject
to change without notice due to air tariffs and currency
exchange fluctuations.

Documents:
All participants are required to have valid passport. U.S.
and Canadian citizens require a visa for travel to Poland,
Hungary, Czechoslovakia and East Berlin. DW Travel
will secure this for you. At this time, no other documents
are required.

Reservations:
Deposit is due now. To make reservations please com-
plete and return to DW Travel the attached form to-
gether with a deposit in the amount of $300.00 per person
made payable to DW Travel. Balance in full will due 60
days prior to departure at which time DW Travel will
send you an invoice.

Cancellations:
In the event of cancellation 90 or more days before de-
parture, full refund will be made less a charge of $50.00
per person for administrative expenses. Between 90 and
60 days prior to departure, refund will be made less a
cancellation charge of $500.00 per person. Cancellation
within 60 days of departure will result in the forfeiture
of the total cost of the trip, land air. There will be
no refunds for absences during the course of the tour.
Insurance will be made available at an additional charge
to protect against charges for cancellation for due cause.

Tour Operations:
Acknowledgment and additional travel information will
be sent to you upon receipt of your deposit by DW Travel,
777 14th Street, N.W., Suite 720, Washington, D.C. 20005
Tel. (202) 639-8804, a fully accredited agency with years
of experience in group travel. DW Travel will also pro-
vide all necessary operations and support to ensure all
participants are well prepared and have an enjoyable tour.

Any changes or cancellations in air reservations from the
set itinerary after tickets have been issued will be subject
to penalties set by the airlines.

sections.conclusion:
In summary, the League would greatly appreciate the Fair Political Practices Commission's opinion on this issue. The League would also appreciate the Commission's views on whether it would strengthen a local official's position for his or her city council to pass a resolution affirming the official nature of the trip and its benefits to the cities who send officials and those which host them.

Unfortunately, the registration deadline for the study mission is June 30, 1990. Accordingly, it would be extremely helpful if the Commission could provide an initial, oral opinion (which the League realizes is non-binding on the Commission) and then follow up such an opinion with a written opinion.

Thank you for the Commission's prompt attention to this request. The League's General Counsel, JoAnne Speers, will contact the Commission on Friday, June 15, 1990 regarding the Commission's initial views on this issue. Please do not hesitate to call her if you have any questions regarding this request.
sections.analysis:
According to the League's research, California Government Code section 85801 allows elected officials to use their campaign funds for expenditures associated with holding office, as long as the expenditures are reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose. See Cal. Gov't Code § 85801. Campaign funds may be used to pay for travel expenses and necessary accommodations as long as these expenditures are "directly related" to a governmental function. See Cal. Gov't Code § 85802(a).

Under the Government Code, members of the elected officials' households may accompany officials to the same destination using campaign funds, if the elected officials may do so. See Cal. Gov't Code § 85802(a)(2) (household members' travel "shall be considered for the same purpose as the . . . elected officer's travel"). The League notes that spouses are always encouraged to accompany local officials to League events, out of a recognition that spouses frequently play an important role in the local officials' discharge of their public and League responsibilities. Moreover, the League's Board of Directors' Manual specifically provides an allowance for travel expenses incurred when the Board President's spouse travels with the President on League business inside or outside of California.

Payments for travel and necessary accommodations are considered "directly related" to a governmental purpose if the payments would meet standards "similar to" the standards in Sections 162 and 274 of the Internal Revenue Code for deductions of travel expenses. See Cal. Gov't Code § 85802(a)(1).

Section 162(a)(2) of the Internal Revenue Code allows taxpayers to deduct traveling expenses, including amounts expended on meals and lodging, while away from home in the pursuit of a trade or business. 26 U.S.C § 162(a)(2). Those traveling expenses which are reasonable and necessary in the conduct of the taxpayer's business and directly attributable to it may be deducted. See 26 C.F.R. § 1.162-2.

The term "trade or business" in the Internal Revenue Code includes the performance of the functions of a public official. 26 U.S.C. § 7701(a)(26). Thus, the Internal Revenue Code allows elected officials to deduct traveling expenses which are reasonable and necessary to their functions as a public official.

Section 274 of the Internal Revenue Code allows deductions for foreign travel for that portion of expenses which is allocable to such trade or business/functions of a public official. See 26 U.S.C. § 274(c)(1) ("... no deduction shall be allowed under section 162 ... for that portion of the expenses of such travel otherwise allowable under such section which, under regulations prescribed by the Secretary, is not allocable to such trade or business [or functions of a public official] ..."). Thus, public officials' travel expenses are deductible under the Internal Revenue Code, as long as the officials follow the allocation rules set forth in section 1.274-4 of title 26 of the Code of Federal Regulations (relating to disallowance of certain kinds of foreign travel expenses).

The Fair Political Practices Commission has recognized in the past that attendance at conferences and educational programs directly relate to the performance of local officials' governmental duties. See Request for Advice No. A-90-210 (March 27, 1990) (Board of Education member may use campaign funds to pay travel and tuition expenses for Harvard's "Program for Senior Executives in State and Local Government"); Request for Advice No. A-90-308 (May 30, 1990) (Board of Education member may use campaign funds for travel, accommodations and conference fees for conference sponsored by both the California and Hawaii middle schools associations on middle schools).

As in those two cases, the League's official study mission provides local officials with an opportunity to expand their horizons on local government issues. In addition, the trip has the philanthropic purpose of enabling California's local officials to share their experiences and insights on local government issues with officials oversees, many of whom are grappling with these issues for the first time. The mission is therefore a natural extension of the League's motto that "California Cities Work Together."

The League would also appreciate the Commission's views on whether it would strengthen a local official's position for his or her city council to pass a resolution affirming the official nature of the trip and its benefits to the cities who send officials and those which host them.

Unfortunately, the registration deadline for the study mission is June 30, 1990. Accordingly, it would be extremely helpful if the Commission could provide an initial, oral opinion (which the League realizes is non-binding on the Commission) and then follow up such an opinion with a written opinion.

Thank you for the Commission's prompt attention to this request. The League's General Counsel, JoAnne Speers, will contact the Commission on Friday, June 15, 1990 regarding the Commission's initial views on this issue. Please do not hesitate to call her if you have any questions regarding this request.
